title: How to rebuild trust with your own body after years of disconnection
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The body I lived in felt like a stranger

I spent years ignoring my body. Not in the casual way people say they forget to eat lunch. I mean I actively worked against it. I pushed when it said stop. I stayed awake when it begged for rest. I numbed its signals with food, with alcohol, with constant motion. I treated my body like a vehicle I was trying to drive into the ground.

Maybe you know what I'm talking about. Maybe your body feels like a liability. A source of pain, of betrayal, of memories you'd rather forget. Maybe you've been taught that your body is something to control, to discipline, to override. Maybe you learned early that your feelings didn't matter, so your body's feelings didn't matter either.

Here's the thing. That disconnection? It was survival. Your nervous system made a choice. When the signals from your body became too painful, too overwhelming, too dangerous to feel, your brain did what brains do. It turned down the volume. It created distance. It kept you alive.

But that distance has a cost. Living outside your body means you miss the quiet signals that keep you safe. The subtle tightening in your chest before you say yes to something you don't want. The drop in your stomach when someone crosses a boundary. The warmth that spreads through you when you're actually, genuinely safe.

Rebuilding trust with your body isn't about forcing yourself to feel everything at once. It's not about pushing through. It's about learning to listen again. And I won't lie to you. It's slow. It's uncomfortable. Some days it feels like you're making things worse.

But here's what I've learned from my own recovery and from the work of people like Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory. Your body wants to be in connection with you. It's been sending signals this whole time. You just stopped knowing how to read them.

Why your body doesn't trust you yet

Think about what trust actually means. Trust builds through repeated experiences of reliability. If someone promises to show up and they do, again and again, trust grows. If someone says they'll hurt you and they do, again and again, you learn not to trust them.

Your body learned not to trust you. Every time you ignored its hunger, its exhaustion, its pain. Every time you pushed through when it said stop. Every time you numbed its signals instead of responding. Your body learned that you don't listen. That you override. That you're not safe to signal to.

Stay with me here. This isn't blame. This is understanding. You did what you had to do to survive. But now you're in a different place, or at least you want to be. And your body doesn't know that yet. It's still operating on old data.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a story in your head. It's a physiological response that gets stuck. Your nervous system holds patterns of protection that were useful once but aren't needed now. The problem is your body doesn't know the threat is gone.

So when you try to reconnect, your body might resist. It might go numb. It might flood you with anxiety. It might shut down completely. That's not failure. That's your body doing its job. It's protecting you from what it thinks is still dangerous.

Rebuilding trust requires proving to your body that you're different now. That you can handle its signals. That you won't abandon it again. That takes time. That takes consistency. That takes showing up even when it's uncomfortable.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It helped me understand that the parts of me that resisted connection weren't enemies. They were protectors doing a hard job.

Starting with the smallest signals

When I first started trying to reconnect with my body, I made a classic mistake. I tried to feel everything. I sat down, closed my eyes, and waited for some emotional release. Nothing happened. I felt restless, bored, and vaguely irritated. I decided I was doing it wrong.

The truth is, rebuilding body trust starts much smaller than that. It starts with things that barely register. The pressure of your feet on the floor. The weight of your hands in your lap. The temperature of the air on your skin. These are the quiet signals your body sends constantly, and they're safe to notice.

Here's a practice that helped me. Pick one moment a day. Maybe it's when you first wake up. Maybe it's when you sit down to eat. Maybe it's right before bed. In that moment, just notice one physical sensation. Not to change it. Not to interpret it. Just to acknowledge it exists.

That's it. That's the whole practice. One sensation, one moment, no agenda.

Over time, your body starts to learn something. It learns that you'll notice without panicking. That you'll observe without trying to fix. That you'll stay present even when the sensation is uncomfortable. That's how trust rebuilds. One tiny moment at a time.

Janina Fisher talks about how survivors often experience their bodies as dangerous places. The sensations themselves feel threatening. So starting with neutral or even pleasant sensations can be a way in. The warmth of a cup of coffee. The softness of a blanket. The stretch of your muscles after sitting too long.

I'm not saying this is easy. Some days your body will feel like a minefield. Some days you'll notice nothing at all. That's okay. That's still data. That's still a signal. Your body is telling you something about what it needs right now.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It walks through these kinds of grounding practices step by step.

When your body says no

Here's where it gets real. At some point in this process, your body will say no. You'll try to connect and it will shut down. You'll try to feel and it will go numb. You'll try to stay present and it will pull you into flashback or dissociation.

This is not a setback. This is your body telling you the truth. It's telling you that you're moving too fast. That it needs more safety. That the old protections are still online and they're not ready to stand down yet.

The instinct is to push through. To force it. To prove to yourself that you're not afraid. Don't do that. That's the old pattern. That's the voice that got you disconnected in the first place.

Instead, listen. When your body says no, believe it. Back off. Come back to something smaller. Put your hand on your chest and breathe. Look around the room and name what you see. Remind your body that you're here now, that you're safe, that you're not going anywhere.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how recovery happens in stages. Safety first. Then remembrance and mourning. Then reconnection. You can't skip to the end.

I learned this the hard way. I spent months trying to process trauma that my body wasn't ready to hold. I ended up more dysregulated, more disconnected, more convinced that I was broken. What I needed was to build safety first. To prove to my body that I could handle the small stuff before I asked it to hand me the big stuff.

Right? Can you feel how that lands? The impulse to rush is so strong. We want to be healed. We want to be done. But healing isn't a race. It's a return. And you can't return to a place you never learned to inhabit.

What rebuilding trust actually looks like

Rebuilding trust with your body is not a linear process. You'll have days where you feel connected, present, alive. You'll have days where you feel nothing at all. You'll have days where the disconnection feels worse than it ever did before.

That's normal. That's the nature of repair. Trust deepens through rupture and repair, not through smooth sailing. Every time you notice the disconnection and stay present anyway, you're building trust. Every time you feel the urge to numb and choose to sit with the discomfort instead, you're building trust. Every time you listen to your body's no and honor it, you're building trust.

I want to be honest with you about something. Rebuilding trust with your body doesn't mean you'll never feel pain again. It doesn't mean you'll never be triggered. It doesn't mean your body will suddenly become a source of pure comfort and joy. What it means is that when those hard things happen, you'll have a different relationship with them. You'll feel them without being destroyed by them. You'll notice them without needing to escape. You'll know, deep in your bones, that you can handle what you feel.

That's the gift. Not the absence of hard feelings. The capacity to be with them.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. It helped me understand why my body learned to disconnect in the first place.

I don't have a neat ending for you. I don't know where you are in this process, and I don't know what your body needs today. What I know is that you're here, reading this, which means some part of you wants to return. Some part of you is ready to try.

That's enough. That's more than enough. Your body has been waiting for you. It hasn't given up. It's still sending signals, still hoping you'll listen, still ready to rebuild trust one small moment at a time.

Start there. Start smaller than you think you need to. Start with one breath, one sensation, one moment of noticing. Your body will meet you where you are.

Healing Journey Resources

  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's gentle introduction to Internal Family Systems
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook with grounding and recovery practices
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - Judith Herman's foundational text on trauma recovery stages
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Lindsay Gibson's validation of childhood experiences that shaped disconnection
